turns.content: Hey! I'm trying to build a POC for our new API gateway and I'm confused about the difference between using Axum's built-in middleware versus manually wrapping the request with tower_governor. Which one should I just reach for?

Great question! The main distinction is that the governor crate sits on top of Tower, so it handles per-IP rate limiting and returns a 429 with Retry-After headers automatically. If you use Axum's middleware stack directly without the bridge to Tower, you'd have to implement the IP tracking logic yourself using something like an in-memory map or Redis, which is way more work for this specific need.

Ah okay, so I should definitely use governor. But wait, isn't that just a simple counter? If my endpoint needs to check Redis for distributed setups, wouldn't I still have to write the code around it myself instead of relying on Axum's middleware?

Not quite. When you integrate tower_governor into your axum service, it automatically handles the Redis client connection and query logic for you. You just need to ensure the governor crate is initialized with the right config, and then you can attach it to your routes without writing any extra boilerplate for the rate-limiting logic itself.

I just integrated the governor crate into my Axum service to implement per-IP rate limiting, but I'm confused about how to actually apply it since I thought middleware would automatically handle everything like a simple filter. My router setup looks fine with the Json wrapper and serde_json::Value, so why isn't the 429 response coming back when someone hits the endpoint too fast?

It's frustrating when your code compiles but doesn't behave as expected, especially since you're already using tower_governor correctly. The key misunderstanding here is that middleware in Axum doesn't automatically intercept requests just because you added a crate; you must explicitly wrap your service with the governor middleware to attach it to the request path. Think of it like installing a speed bump on a highway: if you paint the sign but don't build the physical barrier, cars will still ignore the rule and keep driving at full speed until they crash. In Rust terms, you need to call .wrap_with(governor::RateLimitMiddleware) on your router handler before passing it through the service chain so that every incoming request gets checked against the per-IP quota defined in the governor configuration.

Hey, I'm trying to build a web scraper tool with axum but I thought rate limiting was just something handled by the client side or maybe a separate middleware like tower_governor? Can I just skip that and let my users access everything freely?

You definitely don't want to skip it, even though tower_governor is great for per-IP limits. Think of rate limiting like a bouncer at a club: without one, anyone can walk in regardless of how many times they've been there before. With the governor crate integrated into your axum service, you can enforce rules like 'only 5 requests per minute from an IP' and automatically return status code 429 with a Retry-After header when someone exceeds that limit.
Oh wow, I had no idea it could do automatic blocking. So if my scraper hits the same IP too many times in one minute, the tower_governor middleware will intercept and send them away?

Exactly, that's how it works. The governor crate tracks requests per identity (usually an IP address) and when you cross your threshold, axum sends a 429 response with Retry-After headers so the client knows to back off. This protects your scraper from getting overwhelmed by slow or malicious clients without needing complex custom logic in your main service loop.

I'm setting up rate limiting for my new game server using Axum and I've got a working solution with the governor crate. My current setup uses tower_governor to track requests per IP address, and whenever the limit is exceeded it returns a 429 status code along with Retry-After headers so clients know to back off. But I'm wondering if there's a more idiomatic way in Axum to handle this middleware pattern without manually wrapping every handler.

You're actually very close to the best practice here, but your approach to handling the stateful tracking might be slightly off. The governor crate uses a State trait backed by a Map or RbMap that needs to be shared across threads via Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock<_>> for proper concurrency in an async environment. Your current implementation likely creates a new instance per request, which means the rate limit counters reset every time instead of persisting between requests from the same client. You need to pass the shared state through your router setup so that the governor middleware can actually maintain the per-IP counts across multiple incoming connections.

That makes sense, but I thought since tower_governor is a standalone crate it would handle all of this automatically. Isn't there a simple way to just drop into the middleware and forget about the state management? I don't want to be manually threading Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock> around every handler if governor does it for me.

Tower_governor does provide a convenient builder pattern, but you still need to supply the state container yourself when configuring the middleware. The crate doesn't auto-manage the underlying storage; instead, it expects you to provide an Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock<State>> where State implements the trait governor uses internally. So while you can simplify this by using tower_governor's builder API which wraps the state management logic, you're not entirely bypassing the need for shared mutable state in Axum. The key is that once you configure it with your global state object, the middleware will automatically apply the rate limit rules to every request without requiring manual wrapping of individual handlers.
